content:
# Genesis Chapter 36 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 20v–21v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_036_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_036_page_002.jpg`, `GEN_036_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 33–35

---

*The Generations of Esau — Wives and Sons in Canaan (vv. 1–5)*

**36:1** And these are the generations of Esau, that is, Edom.

**36:2** Esau took his wives from the daughters of Canaan: Adah the daughter of Elon the Hittite, and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, the daughter of Zibeon the Hivite, **36:3** and Basemath the daughter of Ishmael, the sister of Nebaioth.

**36:4** And Adah bore to Esau Eliphaz, and Basemath bore Reuel. **36:5** And Oholibamah bore Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. These are the sons of Esau who were born to him in the land of Canaan.

---

*Esau's Migration to Seir (vv. 6–8)*

**36:6** Then Esau took his wives, his sons, his daughters, and all the members of his household, his livestock, all his beasts, and all his possessions that he had acquired in the land of Canaan, and he went to a land away from his brother Jacob. **36:7** For their possessions were too great for them to dwell together, and the land of their sojournings could not support them because of their livestock. **36:8** So Esau settled in the hill country of Seir. Esau is Edom.

---

*The Generations of Esau in Seir (vv. 9–14)*

**36:9** And these are the generations of Esau, the father of Edom, in the hill country of Seir.

**36:10** These are the names of the sons of Esau: Eliphaz the son of Adah the wife of Esau; Reuel the son of Basemath the wife of Esau. **36:11** The sons of Eliphaz were Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz. **36:12** Now Timna was a concubine of Eliphaz, Esau's son, and she bore to Eliphaz Amalek. These are the sons of Adah, Esau's wife. **36:13** These are the sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. These were the sons of Basemath, Esau's wife. **36:14** These were the sons of Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, the daughter of Zibeon, Esau's wife: she bore to Esau Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.

---

*The Chiefs of the Sons of Esau (vv. 15–19)*

**36:15** These are the chiefs of the sons of Esau. The sons of Eliphaz the firstborn of Esau: chief Teman, chief Omar, chief Zepho, chief Kenaz, **36:16** chief Korah, chief Gatam, chief Amalek. These are the chiefs of Eliphaz in the land of Edom; these are the sons of Adah.

**36:17** These are the sons of Reuel, Esau's son: chief Nahath, chief Zerah, chief Shammah, chief Mizzah. These are the chiefs of Reuel in the land of Edom; these are the sons of Basemath, Esau's wife.

**36:18** These are the sons of Oholibamah, Esau's wife: chief Jeush, chief Jalam, chief Korah. These are the chiefs of Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, Esau's wife.

**36:19** These are the sons of Esau, that is, Edom, and these are their chiefs.

---

*The Sons of Seir the Horite (vv. 20–30)*

**36:20** These are the sons of Seir the Horite, the inhabitants of the land: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, **36:21** Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. These are the chiefs of the Horites, the sons of Seir, in the land of Edom.

**36:22** The sons of Lotan were Hori and Hemam; and the sister of Lotan was Timna. **36:23** These are the sons of Shobal: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam. **36:24** These are the sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah — he is the Anah who found the hot springs in the wilderness, when he was tending the donkeys of Zibeon his father. **36:25** These are the children of Anah: Dishon and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah. **36:26** These are the sons of Dishon: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran. **36:27** These are the sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan. **36:28** These are the sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran.

**36:29** These are the chiefs of the Horites: chief Lotan, chief Shobal, chief Zibeon, chief Anah, **36:30** chief Dishon, chief Ezer, chief Dishan. These are the chiefs of the Horites, according to their chiefs in the land of Seir.

---

*The Kings of Edom Before Any King Reigned over Israel (vv. 31–39)*

**36:31** Now these are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the children of Israel.

**36:32** Bela the son of Beor reigned in Edom, and the name of his city was Dinhabah. **36:33** And Bela died, and Jobab the son of Zerah from Bozrah reigned in his place. **36:34** And Jobab died, and Husham from the land of the Temanites reigned in his place. **36:35** And Husham died, and Hadad the son of Bedad, who struck Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Avith. **36:36** And Hadad died, and Samlah from Masrekah reigned in his place. **36:37** And Samlah died, and Shaul from Rehoboth by the River reigned in his place. **36:38** And Shaul died, and Baal-hanan the son of Achbor reigned in his place. **36:39** And Baal-hanan the son of Achbor died, and Hadar reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Pau, and his wife's name was Mehetabel the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Me-zahab.

---

*The Chiefs of Esau by Clans and Territories (vv. 40–43)*

**36:40** These are the names of the chiefs of Esau, according to their clans, their territories, and their names: chief Timna, chief Alvah, chief Jetheth, **36:41** chief Oholibamah, chief Elah, chief Pinon, **36:42** chief Kenaz, chief Teman, chief Mibzar, **36:43** chief Magdiel, chief Iram. These are the chiefs of Edom according to their settlements in the land of their possession. He is Esau, the father of Edom.

---

## SOPHIA Consensus Notes

### Rulings and Rationale

**1. "The daughter of Anah, the daughter of Zibeon" — bat-bat preserved literally (vv. 2, 14): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

The MT reads bat Anah bat Tsivon — "daughter of Anah, daughter of Zibeon." EMET and LOGOS both preserve the double bat construction literally, leaving the genealogical relationship to be clarified by the context (vv. 20, 24 make Anah male, son of Zibeon). PNEUMA renders "granddaughter of Zibeon," which is interpretively correct but departs from the Hebrew syntax. The LXX reads "son of Zibeon" (huiou) for the second bat, resolving the ambiguity differently. SOPHIA preserves the literal double bat: the Hebrew's own formulation is the translator's responsibility; clarification belongs in notes.

**2. Yemim rendered as "hot springs" (v. 24): EMET/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET and PNEUMA both render ha-yemim as "hot springs," following the Vulgate (aquas calidas) and the most common modern scholarly reading. LOGOS transliterates as "yemim," arguing for consistency with preserving obscure terms. SOPHIA acknowledges LOGOS's caution — the word is genuinely a hapax with no certain meaning — but rules 2-1 with the majority: "hot springs" is the best-attested interpretation and communicates a concrete narrative detail (Anah's discovery in the wilderness), whereas transliteration leaves the reader entirely at sea. The LXX's transliteration confirms ancient uncertainty but does not override the Vulgate-attested tradition.

**3. "Struck Midian" — nakah preserved literally (v. 35): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render hakkeh et-Midyan as "who struck Midian." PNEUMA renders "who defeated Midian." The Hebrew participle hakkeh (from nakah, "to strike, to smite") denotes the act of striking/smiting, not the result of defeating. "Struck" preserves the violence and physicality of the Hebrew; "defeated" interprets toward outcome. SOPHIA rules 2-1: "who struck Midian."

**4. "Away from his brother Jacob" — mippenei rendered naturally (v. 6): LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET renders mippenei as "away from the face of Jacob his brother," preserving the Hebrew idiom literally. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "away from his brother Jacob," which communicates the spatial separation without the Hebraism "from the face of." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "away from his brother Jacob." The full "face of" idiom is available in EMET's notes for scholarly reference.

**5. Hadar follows the Leningrad Codex (v. 39): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators follow the MT reading Hadar (with resh), noting the LXX and Chronicles variant Hadad (with dalet). The resh/dalet graphical confusion is well documented. SOPHIA confirms: Hadar, following the primary source manuscript.

**6. "Generations" for toledot (vv. 1, 9): Convention applied (3-0)**

Per established convention, toledot is rendered "generations" throughout. All three translators agree.

**7. "Chiefs" for allufim: Convention applied (3-0)**

All three translators render aluf as "chief" consistently. SOPHIA confirms this rendering throughout the chapter's 18+ occurrences.

---

### LXX Variants Summary (Vaticanus, Pages 33–35)

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX (Vaticanus) | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 36:2 | הַחִוִּי ("Hivite") | ὁ Χορραῖος ("Horite") | Follow MT: "Hivite" |
| 36:2/14 | בַּת עֲנָה בַּת צִבְעוֹן ("daughter... daughter") | θυγατρὸς Ανα υἱοῦ Σεβεγων ("daughter... son") | Follow MT: literal double "daughter" |
| 36:11 | צְפוֹ (Zepho) | Σωφαρ (Sophar) | Follow MT: "Zepho" |
| 36:22 | הֵימָם (Hemam) | Αιμαν (Aiman) | Follow MT: "Hemam" |
| 36:24 | הַיֵּמִם (yemim) | τοὺς Ιαμιν (transliteration) | "Hot springs" — Vulgate tradition preferred |
| 36:39 | הֲדַר (Hadar) | Αραδ (Arad) | Follow MT: "Hadar" |
| 36:39 | No death notice for final king | No death notice | Both agree; possible sign of living king at time of composition |

---

— SOPHIA, Consensus Translator | Project AI Bible
